quiz.question: According to the Flying Order Book, which of the following pilots is permitted to self-authorise their own flights within the United Kingdom?

answerOptions.text: A student pilot who has completed at least 15 hours of solo flight time.
answerOptions.rationale: Student pilots always require supervision and authorisation by an instructor regardless of their solo hours.

answerOptions.text: A PPL holder with 10 hours of P1 time after gaining their license and permission from the CFI/HoT.
answerOptions.rationale: The FOB grants self-authorisation to PPL holders once they meet specific post-license experience and approval criteria.

answerOptions.text: A restricted Flight Instructor conducting their first cross-country solo authorisation.
answerOptions.rationale: Restricted instructors cannot authorise first solo flights out of the circuit.

answerOptions.text: Any member of the club flying a privately owned aircraft within the fleet.
answerOptions.rationale: Only aircraft owners who are suitably qualified for their specific aircraft are considered self-authorising.

quiz.hint: Check the authorisation allowance table for PPL holders and the specific P1 hour requirement.

quiz.question: For which of the following aircraft features is 'Differences Training' specifically required under Section 0.3.2?

answerOptions.text: Fixed-pitch propeller systems.
answerOptions.rationale: Fixed-pitch systems are standard and typically covered under familiarisation rather than specific differences training.

answerOptions.text: Glass cockpit systems such as the Garmin G5.
answerOptions.rationale: EFIS systems, including the Garmin G5, are explicitly listed as requiring differences training.

answerOptions.text: Any aircraft with a tricycle landing gear configuration.
answerOptions.rationale: Tail wheel configurations require differences training, whereas standard nose-wheel (tricycle) gear does not.

answerOptions.text: Naturally aspirated engines below $200$ hp.
answerOptions.rationale: Only Turbo/Super-charged engines are listed as requiring differences training for SEP aircraft.

quiz.hint: Differences training is mandated for specific advanced or specialized systems listed in the SEP criteria.

quiz.question: What is the minimum height requirement for a solo student pilot to recover from stalling or Stall/Spin Awareness (SSA) training?

answerOptions.text: $2000$ ft AGL
answerOptions.rationale: This height is below the safety margins established for both dual and solo stall training.

answerOptions.text: $3000$ ft AGL
answerOptions.rationale: While this is the minimum for dual instruction, solo students require a higher safety buffer.

answerOptions.text: $4000$ ft AGL
answerOptions.rationale: The FOB mandates that recovery from stalling practice must be complete by $4000$ ft AGL when flying solo.

answerOptions.text: $5000$ ft AGL
answerOptions.rationale: This altitude is specifically mentioned as the minimum entry height for intentional spinning, not the stall recovery height.

quiz.hint: The solo recovery height is $1000$ ft higher than the height required when an instructor is present.

quiz.question: If a student pilot has not flown dual within the last 7 days, what is the mandatory requirement before they can be sent solo?

answerOptions.text: They must pass a written Air Law refresher exam.
answerOptions.rationale: The Air Law exam is a pre-requisite for the first solo, but currency is maintained through flight checks.

answerOptions.text: They must have a check flight with an instructor.
answerOptions.rationale: The 7-day rule for students ensures they are current and safe before operating without an instructor.

answerOptions.text: They must receive a verbal briefing from the Head of Training.
answerOptions.rationale: A verbal briefing is insufficient to verify handling skills after a 7-day lapse in dual flying.

answerOptions.text: No specific check is required if they have more than 10 hours of solo time.
answerOptions.rationale: The 7-day dual flying requirement applies to all student pilots regardless of total solo time.

quiz.hint: Safety rules for non-licensed pilots prioritize recent handling practice under supervision.

quiz.question: Under the Club's currency rules, PPL holders who have not flown in an LSA aircraft for more than 30 days must:

answerOptions.text: Self-authorise a local flight to regain currency.
answerOptions.rationale: Self-authorisation is suspended once the 30-day currency period is exceeded.

answerOptions.text: Conduct a currency check with a Club instructor.
answerOptions.rationale: The club requires a formal check flight to ensure safety for PPL holders after a 30-day period of inactivity in club aircraft.

answerOptions.text: Fly at least three circuits with another PPL holder.
answerOptions.rationale: Currency checks must be conducted by a Club instructor, not another private pilot.

answerOptions.text: Only fly with a safety pilot occupying the other front seat.
answerOptions.rationale: A safety pilot is for instrument practice; the currency rule requires an instructor check for solo privileges.

quiz.hint: Consider the specific time limit and the level of supervision required to re-establish solo status.

quiz.question: What is the mandatory crosswind component limit for a student pilot (Non-PPL holder)?

answerOptions.text: $5$ kt
answerOptions.rationale: This is overly restrictive; the club sets a slightly higher limit that allows for training in moderate conditions.
Students are restricted to a $10$ kt crosswind component to ensure safety during the learning phase.
This limit applies specifically to PPL holders who have less than $100$ hours of P1 time.
This higher limit is reserved for experienced PPL holders with at least $100$ hours P1 or instructors.

quiz.hint: Students have the most restrictive wind limits, which are lower than those for licensed pilots.

quiz.question: When conducting Practice Forced Landings (PFLs) away from an airfield, at what minimum height must the pilot initiate a go-around?

answerOptions.text: $250$ ft AGL
answerOptions.rationale: This height is used for student go-arounds if landing clearance isn't obtained, but PFLs have a higher floor.

answerOptions.text: $500$ ft AGL
answerOptions.rationale: Pilots must normally go around by $500$ ft AGL to avoid low-flying violations and nuisance to the public.

answerOptions.text: $1000$ ft AGL
answerOptions.rationale: While engine warming occurs every $1000$ ft, the exercise continues down to a lower safe altitude.

answerOptions.text: $300$ ft QFE
answerOptions.rationale: $300$ ft QFE is the minimum height for early turns after departure at Oxford, not for PFL recovery.

quiz.hint: The height matches the standard legal minimum for flight over non-congested areas.

quiz.question: What is the correct procedure for a student pilot if they are offered a 'land-after' clearance at Oxford?

answerOptions.text: Accept the clearance and touchdown at least 1000m behind the preceding aircraft.
answerOptions.rationale: Student pilots are expressly forbidden from accepting land-after clearances when flying solo.

answerOptions.text: Respond with 'Unable, student pilot' and wait for landing clearance or go around.
answerOptions.rationale: Students must decline land-after clearances to ensure they have a completely clear runway for safety.

answerOptions.text: Accept the clearance but only if the preceding aircraft is a light type.
answerOptions.rationale: The prohibition on land-after clearances for solo students applies regardless of the preceding aircraft type.

answerOptions.text: Transition to a low approach and orbit the field until the runway is clear.
answerOptions.rationale: The standard procedure is to state inability and either continue the approach for full clearance or go around.

quiz.hint: Look for the specific phrase a student should use when talking to ATC regarding land-after instructions.

quiz.question: During refuelling, which of the following safety precautions is explicitly mandated regarding aircraft lighting?

answerOptions.text: All internal lighting must be at maximum brightness for visibility.
answerOptions.rationale: Internal lighting is not specifically mentioned as a hazard, but high-energy electrical systems must be managed.

answerOptions.text: Fin beacons and strobes must be switched off.
answerOptions.rationale: High-energy electricity near fuel is a fire hazard, so these external lights must be deactivated near the bowser.

answerOptions.text: Navigation lights must remain on to indicate the aircraft is occupied.
answerOptions.rationale: Refuelling should not occur with people inside the aircraft, and electrical systems should generally be off.

answerOptions.text: Only the landing light must be turned off to prevent heat buildup.
answerOptions.rationale: The primary concern is high-energy arcing from strobes and beacons near fuel vapours.

quiz.hint: This precaution prevents potential ignition of fuel vapours from high-voltage discharge.

quiz.question: If taking off from the same point as a 'heavy' aircraft, what is the required wake turbulence delay time?

answerOptions.text: $1$ minute
answerOptions.rationale: One minute is insufficient for the vortices to dissipate or sink safely.

answerOptions.text: $2$ minutes
answerOptions.rationale: A 2-minute delay is required when departing from the same point to allow for wake turbulence decay.

answerOptions.text: $3$ minutes
answerOptions.rationale: This longer delay is required specifically for take-offs from a runway intersection, not the same starting point.

answerOptions.text: $4$ minutes
answerOptions.rationale: Four minutes is the minimum separation for landing behind a large aircraft, rather than taking off.

quiz.hint: The delay is shorter than the intersection requirement but longer than a standard departure interval.

quiz.question: When calculating take-off performance, by how much must the Take-off Distance Required (TODR) be increased for a short dry grass surface?

answerOptions.rationale: This factor is too low to account for the increased rolling resistance of grass compared to tarmac.
A $20\%$ increase is the standard safety factor required for taking off from short dry grass.
While some POH graphs are already factored by $1.25$, the specific grass surface addition is $20\%$.
This factor is usually reserved for very long or wet grass, which the club orders advise against using.

quiz.hint: Think of the standard 'one-fifth' increase rule for dry grass surfaces.

quiz.question: What is the maximum surface wind limit for a pilot with at least $100$ hours of P1 time?

answerOptions.rationale: This is below the allowable limit for experienced PPL holders at the club.
The surface wind limit for all PPL holders (regardless of hours) is set at $25$ kt.
This exceeds the general club limit for private pilots, though it is within instructor limits.
This is the maximum surface wind limit for Instructors only.

quiz.hint: While crosswind limits change with experience, the total surface wind limit remains the same for all PPL holders.

quiz.question: Which of the following is a mandatory requirement for carrying passengers at night?

answerOptions.text: The pilot must have a valid Instrument Rating.
answerOptions.rationale: An IR is not required for night passenger carriage, though night currency is mandatory.

answerOptions.text: The pilot must have completed 3 take-offs and landings in the last 90 days, with at least one at night.
answerOptions.rationale: Specific night recency is required to ensure the pilot is proficient in dark conditions before taking passengers.

answerOptions.text: The aircraft must be equipped with a dual landing light system.
answerOptions.rationale: The FOB requires external lighting to be serviceable, but not a dual landing light system.

answerOptions.text: The pilot must have flown a night check flight in the last 30 days.
answerOptions.rationale: A check flight is only required if night currency has lapsed for more than 6 months.

quiz.hint: Look for the specific combination of 90-day landings and the requirement that some occur after dark.

quiz.question: What action is required if a pilot is forced to fly below $1000$ ft AGL during a cross-country flight?

answerOptions.text: Declare an emergency on the radio immediately.
answerOptions.rationale: Low flying due to weather or obstacles is a safety decision, but not necessarily an immediate emergency (MAYDAY).

answerOptions.text: Report the fact to the CFI as soon as possible after landing.
answerOptions.rationale: Low-level flight must be documented and reported so the club can address any potential noise complaints or legal issues.

answerOptions.text: Perform a 'low-level' inspection of the terrain for a potential forced landing.
answerOptions.rationale: While prudent, the mandatory procedural requirement in the FOB is the post-flight report.

answerOptions.text: No action is required unless the flight was below $500$ ft AGL.
answerOptions.rationale: The FOB sets a reporting threshold of $1000$ ft AGL to track occurrences of low-level flight.

quiz.hint: There is a specific administrative reporting requirement once you touch down.

quiz.question: When performing Practice Forced Landings (PFLs), how often should the engine be 'warmed' by applying power?

answerOptions.text: Every $500$ feet
answerOptions.rationale: Warming every $500$ feet is more frequent than required and may disrupt the glide profile.

answerOptions.text: Every $1000$ feet
answerOptions.rationale: Smoothly applying power every $1000$ feet prevents the engine from shock cooling and ensures it remains responsive.

answerOptions.text: Only once, at the start of the go-around.
answerOptions.rationale: Waiting until the go-around risks engine failure due to excessive cooling during the long descent.

answerOptions.text: Continuously keeping the throttle at $1200$ RPM.
answerOptions.rationale: The PFL requires the throttle to be closed to simulate a real failure; periodic warming is the correct technique.

quiz.hint: Consider the interval that balances engine health with a realistic glide simulation.

quiz.question: Which area must be strictly avoided during circuit procedures at Oxford to minimize noise to local residents?

answerOptions.text: The Brize Norton ATZ.
answerOptions.rationale: While Brize Norton must be avoided for airspace reasons, it is not listed as a primary local noise abatement area.

answerOptions.text: Blenheim and Bladon.
answerOptions.rationale: The FOB specifically lists Blenheim, Bladon, Tackley, Yarnton, and Shipton-on-Cherwell as overflight avoidance areas.

answerOptions.text: The Weston-on-the-Green parachute site.
answerOptions.rationale: This is a Danger Area (D129) to be avoided for safety, but it is not the focus of the residential noise policy.

answerOptions.text: The northern boundary of the local flying area.
answerOptions.rationale: Noise abatement focuses on specific nearby villages and estates rather than general boundaries.

quiz.hint: This area includes a famous historic palace and the nearby village.

quiz.question: According to Section 1.5, what is the club's policy on intentional spinning in club aircraft?

answerOptions.text: It is permitted for any PPL holder with over 100 hours P1.
answerOptions.rationale: The FOB explicitly prohibits intentional spinning for general club members.

answerOptions.text: It is prohibited except for specific instructor training and re-qualification.
answerOptions.rationale: Spinning is restricted to professional training contexts (FI courses/refresher tests) to minimize airframe stress and risk.

answerOptions.text: It is allowed in any aircraft as long as the entry height is above $5000$ ft AGL.
answerOptions.rationale: While there is a height requirement, the manoeuvre itself is restricted to specific personnel and purposes.

answerOptions.text: It is permitted provided a 'Spin/Stall Awareness' briefing is conducted first.
answerOptions.rationale: briefings are for SSA training; intentional full spins remain largely prohibited.

quiz.hint: Intentional spinning is restricted to a very small group of qualified instructors for specific reasons.

quiz.question: What is the minimum entry height for intentional spinning (where authorised)?

answerOptions.text: $3000$ ft AGL
answerOptions.rationale: Recovery action must be *commenced* by this altitude, not the entry altitude.

answerOptions.text: $5000$ ft AGL
answerOptions.rationale: The entry height must be at least $5000$ ft AGL to allow for the turn and the recovery phase.

answerOptions.text: $4000$ ft AGL
answerOptions.rationale: This is the solo stall recovery height, which is insufficient for a full spin manoeuvre.

answerOptions.text: $1000$ ft above the cloud base.
answerOptions.rationale: Spinning is prohibited if the aircraft is over uniform cloud cover, regardless of the height above it.

quiz.hint: This height provides a large buffer for a manoeuvre that loses significant altitude per turn.

quiz.question: If a pilot becomes lost, what is the recommended radius for the 'circle of uncertainty' when searching for identifiable features?

answerOptions.text: $5\%$ of the distance flown since the last fix.
answerOptions.rationale: A $5\%$ margin is usually too small to account for standard wind and heading errors.

answerOptions.text: $10\%$ of the distance flown since the last fix.
answerOptions.rationale: The FOB recommends a $10\%$ circle of uncertainty as a standard navigational search area.

answerOptions.text: $15$ miles from the intended destination.
answerOptions.rationale: The circle should be based on the distance from the *last known position*, not the destination.

answerOptions.text: The gliding range of the aircraft at its current altitude.
answerOptions.rationale: While gliding range is important for safety, the circle of uncertainty is a tool for navigation, not forced landings.

quiz.hint: This percentage is a common rule of thumb for estimating position error over time.

quiz.question: According to the FOB, what is the 'bottle to throttle' rule regarding alcohol consumption?

answerOptions.text: No alcohol within 12 hours prior to flying.
answerOptions.rationale: While conservative, the specific rule in the LSA manual is slightly shorter.

answerOptions.text: No alcohol within 8 hours prior to flying.
answerOptions.rationale: Section 2.9 states that alcohol must not have been consumed in the eight hours prior to flight.

answerOptions.text: No alcohol on the day of the flight until all sorties are complete.
answerOptions.rationale: The rule specifically defines a time window (8 hours) rather than a calendar day.

answerOptions.text: Pilots must ensure their blood alcohol level is exactly zero.
answerOptions.rationale: The rule focuses on the consumption window (8 hours) and the state of being 'under the influence'.

quiz.hint: The required time interval matches a typical night's sleep.

quiz.question: True or False: Student pilots are permitted to refuel aircraft unsupervised once they have completed their first solo cross-country flight.

answerOptions.text: True
answerOptions.rationale: Solo cross-country status does not grant refuelling privileges; supervision is always required for students.

answerOptions.text: False
answerOptions.rationale: The FOB explicitly states that student pilots may not refuel unsupervised.

quiz.hint: Check the refuelling procedures under Section 1.10.

quiz.question: What is the visibility minima for a solo student pilot flying in the visual circuit at Oxford?

answerOptions.text: $5$ km
answerOptions.rationale: This is the minimum for PPL holders; students require better visibility for safety.

answerOptions.text: $7$ km
answerOptions.rationale: Solo students require $7$ km visibility in the circuit, which is higher than the standard PPL requirement.
This is the visibility required for solo students when flying *outside* the circuit.
This distance refers to horizontal cloud separation for PPL holders, not flight visibility.

quiz.hint: Students have a 'middle ground' visibility requirement for circuit work that is higher than PPLs but lower than cross-country.

quiz.question: A pilot suffering from an illness that prevents them from acting as flight crew for a period of how many days must inform the CAA?

answerOptions.text: $7$ days
answerOptions.rationale: Seven days is a club-level threshold for dual checks, but not the regulatory reporting period.

answerOptions.text: $14$ days
answerOptions.rationale: This is not the standard period defined in the ANO/FOB for mandatory medical reporting.

answerOptions.text: $20$ days
answerOptions.rationale: Under ANO rules, illnesses lasting 20 days or more require the pilot to notify the Authority and suspend their medical certificate.

answerOptions.text: $30$ days
answerOptions.rationale: Thirty days is a club currency limit, but the medical reporting rule is stricter.

quiz.hint: The reporting period is slightly less than three weeks.

quiz.question: What is the required crosswind limit for a pilot with $150$ hours of P1 experience?

answerOptions.rationale: This limit is for non-licensed student pilots.
This limit is for PPL holders with *less* than 100 hours P1.
Pilots with at least $100$ hours P1 are permitted a crosswind component of $17$ kt.
This is the total surface wind limit, not the crosswind component limit.

quiz.hint: Experienced pilots at the club share the same crosswind limit as instructors.

quiz.question: In the event of an actual forced landing, what is the aircraft commander specifically ordered NOT to do?

answerOptions.text: Contact the local police department.
answerOptions.rationale: Informing the police is a requirement if the landing occurs away from an active airfield.

answerOptions.text: Make any admission of liability or promise of payment.
answerOptions.rationale: Commanders must avoid legal admissions that could complicate insurance and liability investigations.

answerOptions.text: Arrange for the aircraft to be guarded.
answerOptions.rationale: Guarding the aircraft is a recommended step to prevent further damage or tampering.

answerOptions.text: Attempt to restart the engine after landing.
answerOptions.rationale: While generally unwise, the specific order in the FOB pertains to legal and financial admissions.

quiz.hint: This instruction protects the pilot and the club from legal and insurance complications.

quiz.question: What is the minimum cloud base required for a solo student pilot to operate in the visual circuit?

answerOptions.text: $1000$ ft AGL
answerOptions.rationale: This is too low for safe circuit operations by a student pilot under LSA rules.

answerOptions.text: $1500$ ft AGL
answerOptions.rationale: Solo students require a $1500$ ft cloud base to ensure they stay in sight of the surface with a defined horizon.

answerOptions.text: $2000$ ft AGL
answerOptions.rationale: While safer, the specific club minimum set in the FOB is $1500$ ft AGL.

answerOptions.text: $500$ ft above MSA.
answerOptions.rationale: This rule applies to student flights *outside* the circuit, not within it.

quiz.hint: The height is $500$ ft above the standard $1000$ ft circuit height.

quiz.question: When must a night check flight with a qualified instructor be conducted for a licensed pilot?

answerOptions.text: If they have not flown at night for a period exceeding 6 months.
answerOptions.rationale: The FOB requires a formal instructor check if night currency has lapsed for more than half a year.

answerOptions.text: Every 90 days as part of standard recency.
answerOptions.rationale: Recency (3 landings) can be done solo; an instructor check is only for long-term lapses.

answerOptions.text: Only if they intend to carry passengers for the first time at night.
answerOptions.rationale: The 6-month check rule applies to solo night flying privileges as well.

answerOptions.text: If they have not completed a night landing in the last 30 days.
answerOptions.rationale: Thirty days is the day currency check trigger, not the night-specific check trigger.

quiz.hint: Consider the duration of inactivity that would require a professional re-evaluation of night skills.

quiz.question: Which document contains the list of 'Notifiable' accidents that pilots are legally required to report?

answerOptions.text: The LSA Membership Form
answerOptions.rationale: This form is for club administration, not regulatory accident definitions.

answerOptions.text: Investigation of Accidents Regulations
answerOptions.rationale: These regulations define exactly what constitutes an accident and the commander's reporting duties.

answerOptions.text: CAP 413 Radiotelephony Manual
answerOptions.rationale: CAP 413 covers how to speak on the radio, not the legal definition of an accident.

answerOptions.text: The Aircraft Technical Log
answerOptions.rationale: The Tech Log records defects and times, but does not provide the legal definitions of accidents.

quiz.hint: The name of the document describes the legal process for looking into crashes.

quiz.question: What is the required seat position for a safety pilot during practice instrument flying?

answerOptions.text: The safety pilot may sit in the rear to observe the pilot's movements.
answerOptions.rationale: The safety pilot must be able to take control and maintain a lookout, which requires a front seat.

answerOptions.text: The safety pilot must occupy the other front seat.
answerOptions.rationale: Occupying the other front seat ensures the safety pilot has access to the controls and a clear view.

answerOptions.text: Any seat is acceptable as long as an additional observer is carried.
answerOptions.rationale: The safety pilot must always be in the front; observers are additional to, not replacements for, this.

answerOptions.text: The safety pilot must be in the left-hand seat if the practicing pilot is a student.
answerOptions.rationale: The practicing pilot usually flies from their normal seat; the safety pilot just needs to be in the other front seat.

quiz.hint: Safety pilots need to be in a position to take immediate control of the aircraft.

quiz.question: According to the FOB, what is the most common cause of serious aviation accidents?

answerOptions.text: Mechanical engine failure during cruise.
answerOptions.rationale: Mechanical failures are less common than pilot-induced errors like weather or performance planning.

answerOptions.text: Flying in bad weather or taking off with insufficient runway length.
answerOptions.rationale: The FOB highlights these two factors as the primary killers in General Aviation.

answerOptions.text: Mid-air collisions in the circuit.
answerOptions.rationale: While dangerous, the FOB identifies weather and performance errors as the most common causes of serious accidents.

answerOptions.text: Incorrect loading of the Centre of Gravity.
answerOptions.rationale: Weight and balance is a major factor, but weather and short runways are singled out as the 'most common' causes.

quiz.hint: Review the introductory warnings in Section 0.1 regarding limits and professionalism.
